settings.headline: Your hot flashes aren't "just aging." Your gut stopped regulating your hormones.
settings.subheadline: Your gut contains a specialized bacterial community called the estrobolome — it controls how your body metabolizes and recycles estrogen through an enzyme called beta-glucuronidase. When these bacteria deplete, estrogen clearance accelerates and hormonal balance breaks down. The hot flashes, mood swings, and weight gain that follow aren't inevitable — they're a signal.
settings.agitate_eyebrow: Why Hormone Supplements Alone Aren't Enough
settings.agitate_headline: You're adding hormones to a system that can't hold onto them. It's a leaky bucket.
settings.agitate_body: You're 38 and waking up drenched at 3am. Hot flashes in meetings. A mood crash that comes out of nowhere and derails your entire afternoon. Your doctor says it's perimenopause. Take these supplements. Try this cream. Wait it out.||The supplement graveyard is growing — DIM, black cohosh, evening primrose oil, maca, vitex, magnesium. Each one helped for a week, then stopped. You're spending $150/month on a stack that keeps shifting because nothing holds. Nobody told you why.||Here's what's missing from the conversation: your gut bacteria regulate how much estrogen your body retains versus excretes. When those bacteria deplete, every hormone supplement you take clears your system too fast. You're pouring water into a bucket with a hole in the bottom. The hole is in your microbiome.
settings.cost_headline: HRT and hormone supplements cost $100-300/month — and don't fix the recycling system. Origin costs $34/month and supports it.
settings.cost_body: Women dealing with hormonal imbalance spend $1,200-3,600 per year on HRT, bioidentical creams, adaptogens, and specialist visits. These can address symptoms, but they don't restore the estrobolome — the bacterial community that determines how your body processes estrogen in the first place. For $1.13/day, Origin supports the gut ecosystem your hormonal system depends on.
settings.bridge_headline: The estrobolome: the hormone regulator hiding in your gut.
settings.bridge_body: Plottel and Blaser coined the term 'estrobolome' in 2011 to describe the collection of gut bacteria capable of metabolizing estrogen. These bacteria produce beta-glucuronidase — the enzyme that determines how much estrogen your body recirculates versus eliminates. Flores et al. demonstrated that gut bacterial diversity directly correlates with urinary estrogen levels. Baker et al. confirmed the clinical connection between microbiome composition and circulating estrogen in the Journal of Clinical Endocrinology & Metabolism. When key bacterial populations deplete, the estrobolome loses function and hormonal symptoms follow.
settings.citation: Plottel & Blaser, Cell Host & Microbe, 2011 · Flores et al., mBio, 2012 · Baker et al., Journal of Clinical Endocrinology & Metabolism, 2017
settings.solution_headline: Origin supports your estrobolome. Your body can handle the rest.
settings.solution_body: L. acidophilus and L. plantarum contribute to beta-glucuronidase activity, supporting your body's natural estrogen recycling process. L. paracasei helps modulate the inflammatory signaling that can disrupt hormonal pathways. B. lactis supports gut barrier integrity — keeping the system sealed so bacterial metabolites reach the right receptors. Origin doesn't replace medical treatment. It supports the gut foundation that helps your body regulate its own hormones more effectively.
settings.lead_headline: The estrobolome research that connects your gut health to your hormonal balance.
settings.closing_quote: The women in your family didn't have a supplement graveyard on the counter. They aged with steadiness, with grace. The difference isn't luck or genetics — it's the microbiome they kept intact through ancestral food.

settings.title: Key bacteria deplete, enzyme production drops
settings.body: Dietary westernization depletes L. acidophilus and L. plantarum — bacteria involved in beta-glucuronidase production. Without adequate enzyme activity, your body clears estrogen faster than it should. The recycling system loses efficiency.

settings.title: Estrogen metabolism shifts, symptoms follow
settings.body: With reduced estrogen recirculation, your body struggles to maintain hormonal balance. Hot flashes, night sweats, mood instability, sleep disruption, and stubborn weight gain around the midsection follow — not because of aging, but because the regulatory system is compromised.

settings.title: Rebuild the bacterial foundation
settings.body: Origin's four strains support the bacterial community your estrobolome relies on. As gut diversity recovers, beta-glucuronidase activity can normalize. Your body gets a better chance to regulate its own hormonal balance — from the gut up.

settings.title: Sleep through the night again
settings.body: Night sweats and hot flashes are downstream of estrogen dysregulation. When the estrobolome has the bacteria it needs, thermoregulation can stabilize and uninterrupted sleep becomes possible again.

settings.title: Steady mood without the rollercoaster
settings.body: Estrogen helps regulate serotonin and other neurotransmitters. When your gut supports healthier estrogen metabolism, the mood swings, irritability, and emotional crashes can ease — and you start feeling like yourself again.

settings.title: Feel like yourself again
settings.body: The mental fog, the fatigue, the feeling of being disconnected from your own body — these aren't permanent. When the gut-hormone connection strengthens, women consistently describe it the same way: 'I feel like me again.'

settings.title: Simplify the supplement stack
settings.body: DIM, black cohosh, maca, vitex, evening primrose, ashwagandha — the stack keeps growing because nothing addresses the upstream cause. Origin supports the gut foundation so your body can do more of the hormonal regulation on its own.
